content.explanation:
Error handling is one of the most critical aspects of writing robust JavaScript applications. The try/catch/finally statement provides a structured way to handle runtime errors gracefully instead of letting them crash your program. When code inside a try block throws an error, execution immediately jumps to the catch block, where you can inspect the error and decide how to respond.

The basic syntax wraps potentially dangerous code in a try block and provides a catch block that receives the error object. The catch block's parameter gives you access to the error that was thrown, allowing you to log it, display a user-friendly message, or attempt recovery. Without try/catch, an unhandled error would propagate up the call stack and potentially terminate your application or leave it in an inconsistent state.

The finally block is a powerful addition that executes regardless of whether an error occurred. This makes it ideal for cleanup operations like closing database connections, releasing file handles, or hiding loading spinners. The finally block runs after both the try and catch blocks complete, and it runs even if either block contains a return statement. This guarantee makes finally indispensable for resource management.

Nested try/catch blocks allow you to handle errors at different granularity levels. An inner try/catch can handle specific errors while letting unexpected ones propagate to an outer handler. This pattern is common in complex operations where different steps might fail in different ways and require different recovery strategies.

The error object in JavaScript contains three standard properties: message (a human-readable description), name (the error type like 'TypeError' or 'RangeError'), and stack (a string showing the call stack at the point the error was created). The stack trace is invaluable for debugging because it shows exactly where the error originated and the chain of function calls that led to it.

Rethrowing errors is a technique where you catch an error, perform some action (like logging), and then throw it again so that a higher-level handler can deal with it. This is especially useful when you want to add context or perform side effects without fully handling the error. With async/await, try/catch becomes the primary way to handle rejected promises, replacing the .catch() chain pattern and making asynchronous error handling look synchronous.

codeExamples.title: Basic try/catch/finally
codeExamples.output:
Parsed successfully: { name: 'Alice' }
Parse attempt completed
Error name: SyntaxError
Error message: Unexpected token 'i', "invalid json" is not valid JSON
Parse attempt completed
codeExamples.explanation: The first call parses valid JSON and returns the object. The second call fails, triggering the catch block which logs the error details. In both cases, the finally block runs, confirming the attempt completed.

codeExamples.title: Nested try/catch and rethrowing
codeExamples.output:
Inner catch: Name is required
Outer catch: Name is required
Stack preview: Error: Name is required
---
Inner catch: Unexpected token 'b', "bad data" is not valid JSON
Outer catch: Unexpected token 'b', "bad data" is not valid JSON
codeExamples.explanation: The inner try/catch handles the immediate parsing/validation, logs the error, then rethrows so the outer handler can also process it. This layered approach allows different levels of error handling.

codeExamples.title: finally with return statements
codeExamples.output:
Trying operation...
Caught: Something went wrong
Cleanup: closing resources
Result: recovered
codeExamples.explanation: Even though the catch block returns 'recovered', the finally block still executes before the function actually returns. If finally also had a return, it would override the catch block's return value - a common pitfall.

codeExamples.title: try/catch with async/await
codeExamples.explanation: With async/await, try/catch handles rejected promises the same way it handles synchronous errors. The catch block can differentiate error types - network failures produce TypeErrors, while HTTP errors can be thrown manually with custom messages.

notes: The finally block always executes, even if try or catch contains a return, throw, or break statement.
You can have try/finally without a catch block when you only need cleanup without error handling.
In modern JavaScript, the catch binding is optional: you can write catch { } without (error) if you don't need the error object (ES2019).
Errors thrown inside a catch block will NOT be caught by the same try/catch - they propagate to the next outer handler.
commonPitfalls: Swallowing errors silently with an empty catch block - always at minimum log the error for debugging.
Putting a return statement in a finally block - it overrides returns from both try and catch, leading to confusing behavior.
Forgetting that try/catch only catches synchronous errors unless used with await - a rejected promise without await won't be caught.
Using try/catch around code that can't actually throw (like simple variable assignments) adds unnecessary overhead.
interviewTips: Be prepared to explain the execution order of try/catch/finally, especially when return statements are in multiple blocks.
Know that finally runs before the function actually returns, even though the return value is already determined.
Understand that try/catch is synchronous by nature - for promises, you need either .catch() or await inside try/catch.

exercises.title: Safe JSON Parser
exercises.description: Write a function `safeParseJSON` that takes a string, tries to parse it with `JSON.parse`, and returns the parsed object. If parsing fails, catch the error and return `null`. Use a `finally` block to log 'Parse attempted' every time. Test with a valid JSON string and an invalid one.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Parse attempted
Result 1: {"x":42}
Parse error: Unexpected token 'o', "not json" is not valid JSON
Parse attempted
Result 2: null
hints: JSON.parse throws a SyntaxError when the input string is not valid JSON
The finally block runs regardless of whether try succeeded or catch was triggered

exercises.title: Nested Error Handling with Rethrow
exercises.description: Write a function `processValue` that takes a value. In an inner try/catch, check if the value is a number. If not, throw a TypeError with 'Expected a number'. If it is negative, throw a RangeError with 'Must be positive'. In the inner catch, log 'Inner: <error.name>' and rethrow. In the outer catch, log 'Outer: <error.message>'. If valid, log 'Value: <value>'.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Value: 10
Inner: TypeError
Outer: Expected a number
Inner: RangeError
Outer: Must be positive
hints: Use typeof val !== 'number' to check the type, and throw new TypeError(...)
Rethrowing means catching the error, doing something, then using throw again to pass it up

content.explanation:
JavaScript provides several built-in error types, each representing a specific category of problem. Understanding these types helps you diagnose issues faster and write more targeted error handling. The base Error class is the parent of all error types, and each subclass communicates a different kind of failure to both developers and error-handling code.

TypeError is the most common error type in JavaScript. It occurs when a value is not of the expected type - for example, calling a non-function, accessing properties on null or undefined, or passing the wrong argument type to a built-in method. ReferenceError fires when you try to use a variable that hasn't been declared. SyntaxError typically appears at parse time when the JavaScript engine encounters malformed code, though it can also occur at runtime with functions like JSON.parse() or eval(). RangeError indicates a numeric value is outside its allowed range, like creating an array with a negative length or calling toFixed() with too many digits. URIError is rare and only occurs when URI handling functions like decodeURIComponent() receive malformed input.

Creating custom error classes is essential for building maintainable applications. By extending the built-in Error class, you create domain-specific error types that make your error handling more precise and self-documenting. A custom error class can carry additional properties like HTTP status codes, error codes, or user-facing messages, making it easier for catch blocks to decide how to respond.

The key to a proper custom error class is calling super(message) in the constructor to set the message property, and setting the name property to match your class name. Without setting name, your error will show up as 'Error' in stack traces instead of your custom class name, making debugging harder. You should also ensure the prototype chain is correct so that instanceof checks work properly.

Building an error hierarchy allows you to catch errors at different levels of specificity. For example, you might have a base AppError, with subclasses like ValidationError, AuthenticationError, and NotFoundError. A catch block can check for the most specific type first and fall back to broader types, providing appropriate responses at each level.

The instanceof operator is the standard way to check error types in catch blocks. It works with both built-in and custom error types and respects the inheritance chain, so catching a parent error type also catches all its subclasses. This makes hierarchical error handling clean and maintainable, and it's far more reliable than checking the error's name string property.

codeExamples.title: Built-in error types in action
codeExamples.output:
TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading 'toString')
ReferenceError: undeclaredVar is not defined
RangeError: Invalid array length
URIError: URI malformed
codeExamples.explanation: Each built-in error type signals a specific category of problem. Recognizing these types helps you understand what went wrong without even reading the message.

codeExamples.title: Creating custom error classes
codeExamples.output:
ValidationError
Invalid input
400
[ 'email', 'password' ]
true
true
true
codeExamples.explanation: Custom errors extend the Error class and can carry domain-specific data. The inheritance chain works correctly - a ValidationError is also an AppError and an Error, making hierarchical catch blocks possible.

codeExamples.title: Error hierarchy in catch blocks
codeExamples.output:
Connection failed to db.example.com - retrying...
DB error on query: SELECT * FROM users
App error [GENERIC]: Something broke
Unknown error: Random error
codeExamples.explanation: The instanceof checks go from most specific to most general. A ConnectionError matches the first check even though it's also a DatabaseError and AppError. Order matters - putting AppError first would catch everything.

codeExamples.title: Practical error handling pattern
codeExamples.output:
Validation failed
  name: Name must be at least 2 characters
  email: Valid email is required
  age: Age must be between 0 and 150
codeExamples.explanation: This real-world pattern collects all validation errors before throwing a single ValidationError with structured error data. The catch block can then display field-specific messages to the user.

notes: Always set the name property in custom error classes - it appears in stack traces and helps with debugging.
The instanceof operator respects the prototype chain, so a child error will match instanceof checks for all parent classes.
SyntaxError from eval() or JSON.parse() is the only SyntaxError you can catch at runtime - parser-level syntax errors halt execution before any code runs.
Custom errors can be serialized to JSON for API responses by adding a toJSON() method.
commonPitfalls: Forgetting to call super(message) in the constructor - this leaves the message property empty and breaks the stack trace.
Checking error.name === 'TypeError' instead of using instanceof - name strings can be spoofed and don't respect inheritance.
Not setting the name property on custom errors - they'll show as 'Error' in stack traces instead of your custom name.
Ordering instanceof checks from general to specific - the first match wins, so a parent class check would catch all child errors.
interviewTips: Know all six built-in error types and when each one occurs - this is a common JavaScript trivia question.
Be able to write a custom error class from scratch with proper inheritance and demonstrate instanceof checking.
Understand that Error.captureStackTrace (V8 only) can be used to exclude the constructor from the stack trace for cleaner output.

exercises.title: Create a Custom Error Class
exercises.description: Create a `ValidationError` class that extends `Error`. It should accept a `message` and a `field` parameter. Set the `name` to 'ValidationError'. Then throw and catch it, logging the name, message, and field. Also verify it is an instance of both `ValidationError` and `Error`.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Name: ValidationError
Message: Invalid email
Field: email
Is ValidationError: true
Is Error: true
hints: Call super(message) first in the constructor to set the message property
Set this.name to match your class name so stack traces show the correct error type

exercises.title: Error Hierarchy with instanceof
exercises.description: Build a small error hierarchy: `AppError` (base, with a `code` property), `NotFoundError` (extends AppError, sets code to 'NOT_FOUND'), and `AuthError` (extends AppError, sets code to 'UNAUTHORIZED'). Write a `handleError` function that uses instanceof to identify the error type and log a specific message for each. Test with all three error types.
exercises.expectedOutput:
Not found: User not found
Auth error: Authentication required
App error [GENERIC]: Something broke
hints: Order instanceof checks from most specific to most general -- child classes first
NotFoundError and AuthError are subclasses of AppError, so they must be checked before AppError

content.explanation:
Debugging is the systematic process of finding and fixing errors in your code. While it might seem like an art, effective debugging follows predictable patterns and leverages powerful tools built into every modern browser. Mastering these techniques will dramatically reduce the time you spend hunting down bugs and make you a more productive developer.

The console object offers far more than just console.log(). The console.table() method displays arrays and objects in a clean tabular format, making it easy to compare data at a glance. console.group() and console.groupEnd() let you organize related log statements into collapsible sections, which is invaluable when debugging loops or recursive functions. console.time() and console.timeEnd() measure execution time with millisecond precision, helping you identify performance bottlenecks without external profiling tools.

Browser DevTools are the most powerful debugging environment available to JavaScript developers. The Sources panel lets you set breakpoints by clicking on line numbers, and execution pauses at those points so you can inspect every variable in scope. The Call Stack panel shows how you arrived at the current line, and the Scope panel displays all accessible variables organized by scope level. You can also set conditional breakpoints that only pause when a specific expression evaluates to true, which is essential for debugging issues that only occur with certain data.

The debugger statement is a programmatic breakpoint that you place directly in your code. When DevTools is open and execution reaches a debugger statement, the browser pauses just like it would at a manual breakpoint. This is particularly useful for debugging code that runs in callbacks, event handlers, or dynamically loaded scripts where setting breakpoints in the UI might be difficult. Remember to remove debugger statements before committing your code.

Step-through debugging is the process of executing code one line at a time while observing state changes. DevTools provides four stepping controls: Step Over (execute the current line and move to the next), Step Into (enter the function being called), Step Out (finish the current function and return to the caller), and Resume (continue until the next breakpoint). Learning when to use each stepping mode is key to efficient debugging.

Reading stack traces is a fundamental skill. A stack trace shows the chain of function calls that led to an error, with the most recent call at the top. Each line typically shows the function name, file name, and line number. By reading from top to bottom, you can trace the execution path and identify where things went wrong. Combined with source maps, stack traces work even with minified or transpiled code in production environments.

codeExamples.title: Console methods beyond log
codeExamples.output:
┌─────────┬───────────┬─────┬─────────────┐
│ (index) │   name    │ age │    role     │
├─────────┼───────────┼─────┼─────────────┤
│    0    │  'Alice'  │  28 │ 'Developer' │
│    1    │   'Bob'   │  34 │ 'Designer'  │
│    2    │ 'Charlie' │  22 │  'Intern'   │
└─────────┴───────────┴─────┴─────────────┘
User Processing
  Loading users...
  Validation
    Checking names...
    Checking emails...
  Processing complete
arrayOperation: 3.456ms
fruit: 1
vegetable: 1
fruit: 2
fruit: 3
codeExamples.explanation: console.table renders arrays as formatted tables. console.group creates collapsible sections. console.time measures execution duration. console.count tracks how many times each label is logged - great for verifying function call frequency.

codeExamples.title: The debugger statement
codeExamples.explanation: The debugger statement acts as a programmatic breakpoint. When the browser's DevTools is open, execution pauses at that line, letting you inspect all variables in scope and step through the remaining code line by line.

codeExamples.title: Reading stack traces
codeExamples.output:
Error: Something failed in inner function

Stack trace:
Error: Something failed in inner function
at innerFunction (script.js:2:9)
at middleFunction (script.js:6:3)
at outerFunction (script.js:10:3)
codeExamples.explanation: The stack trace reads top-to-bottom from where the error was thrown back to the original caller. Line 2 in innerFunction is where the error originated, called from middleFunction at line 6, which was called from outerFunction at line 10.

codeExamples.title: Practical debugging strategies
codeExamples.output_note: console.assert output may vary by environment
codeExamples.explanation: Four practical strategies: (1) Place strategic logs to narrow down where values go wrong. (2) Use console.assert to validate assumptions - it only logs when the condition is false. (3) JSON.stringify with indentation reveals deeply nested objects that console.log truncates. (4) Label prefixes like [AUTH] or [DB] let you filter logs by category.

notes: console.table() also accepts a second argument - an array of column names to display, filtering out columns you don't need.
Use console.dir(element) instead of console.log(element) for DOM elements to see the JavaScript object properties instead of the HTML.
Chrome DevTools allows logpoints - breakpoints that log a message without pausing execution, useful for production-like debugging.
The Network tab in DevTools is essential for debugging API calls - check request headers, payloads, and response data.
commonPitfalls: Leaving debugger statements and console.log calls in production code - use a linter rule (no-debugger, no-console) to catch these.
Relying only on console.log instead of using breakpoints - stepping through code reveals issues that logging might miss.
Logging objects by reference - by the time you expand a logged object in the console, its values may have changed. Use JSON.stringify() or spread to capture the current state.
Ignoring the browser console warnings and errors - browsers often provide helpful hints about deprecations, performance issues, and accessibility problems.
interviewTips: Familiarize yourself with all console methods - interviewers sometimes ask about lesser-known ones like console.trace(), console.assert(), and console.dir().
Know the difference between stepping over, stepping into, and stepping out of a function in the debugger.
Be able to explain how source maps work - they map minified/transpiled code back to the original source for debugging in production.

exercises.title: Using console.assert for Validation
exercises.description: Write a function `validateAge` that takes a value and uses `console.assert` to check three conditions: the value is a number, it is not negative, and it is not greater than 150. If all assertions pass, log 'Valid age: <value>'. Test with a valid age (25), a negative age (-5), and a non-number ('abc').
exercises.expectedOutput:
Valid age: 25
Assertion failed: age cannot be negative
Assertion failed: value must be a number
hints: Check typeof value !== 'number' first to catch non-number inputs
Use early returns after each failed check so subsequent checks don't run on invalid data

exercises.title: Stack Trace Inspection
exercises.description: Create three nested functions: `outer` calls `middle`, which calls `inner`. `inner` throws an Error with message 'Bug found!'. Catch the error in `outer` and log the error message and the first three lines of the stack trace (name of each function from the stack).
exercises.expectedOutput:
Error: Bug found!
Call stack:
  -> inner
  -> middle
  -> outer
hints: error.stack is a string with newline-separated lines; split it with split('\n')
Each stack line typically contains 'at functionName' which you can extract with a regex or string methods
